narrative: Tata Steel’s FY2024 standalone comparison gives a clearer and tougher view than FY2023. It ranked first on 26 indicators but fourth on 38, showing a strong strategic platform but weak relative outcomes in several operating ESG metrics. Tata was the financial leader, with the highest turnover and net worth, and remained the best workforce-diversity performer by female employee share and female wage share. It also sustained 100% training coverage and led value-chain environmental assessment at 74%, supported by a Responsible Supply Chain Policy covering health and safety, fair business practices, environmental protection and human rights. Its R&D and capex disclosures were strategically strong: 100% of R&D spend was aligned to NGRBC principles, and sustainability capex included CO2, SOx, NOx, dust reduction, water conservation, effluent treatment, waste utilisation, safety and employee welfare. The weaknesses were material. Tata ranked last on Scope 1, Scope 2, Scope 3 and physical GHG intensity, with 3.1 tCO2e/tcs, and also lagged on water withdrawal, water consumption, waste disposal, POSH complaints and all core safety frequency indicators. Circularity remained a strength, including 4.3 million tonnes of scrap recycled in FY2023-24, but landfill and regulated-waste outcomes weakened the environmental story. FY2024 therefore shows Tata as the most institutionally mature player, but not the operational ESG benchmark.
